  1. Biostatistics
  2. Part II. Core hypothesis tests
  3. 10. Routes on the Map of the Statistical Landscape

10. Routes on the Map of the Statistical Landscape

A Practical Decision Guide for Core Inferential Questions

Author
Affiliation

A. J. Smit

University of the Western Cape

Published

2026/03/22

NoteIn This Chapter
  • how to connect the biological question to the method family
  • choosing among the core methods already introduced
  • a visual decision aid for moving from question to method
  • when to consider non-parametric alternatives
  • when the problem has moved beyond staple tests and into modelling
ImportantTasks to Complete in This Chapter
  • None

1 Introduction

Many students first encounter statistics as a list of named tests. In practice, method selection starts with the biological question, the structure of the data, and the design of the study.

By this point you have already met the core tests. In your working life, you will have to choose among them and recognise when the problem has gone into a broader model class. But, test choice is only the first step. Good interpretation still depends on effect size, uncertainty, and biological context.

2 The Decision Workflow

Use these steps first. The later sections provide two complementary ways to navigate it.

  1. State the biological question.
  2. Identify the response variable and its type.
  3. Identify the explanatory structure: one sample, groups, paired data, or continuous predictor.
  4. Decide whether the observations are independent, paired, repeated, or nested.
  5. Choose the method family that matches the structure.
  6. Check whether the assumptions of that family are plausible.
  7. Interpret the result in terms of effect size, uncertainty, and the scientific question.

Test selection depends on the question, the data structure, the design, and the assumptions together, and no single R function can replace that decision flow order.

3 Visual Decision Assistance

The workflow is summarised in Figure 1. Each row in the figure goes directly onto the categories developed in the next two sections.

4 Choosing by Data Structure

Usually, your data structure, often set at the experimental or sampling design stage, will show whether the simple core tests are enough or whether you need regression, GLMs, or mixed models.

4.1 Continuous Response with a Categorical Predictor

This is the comparison-of-means family.

  • one sample against a reference value → one-sample t-test;
  • two independent groups → two-sample t-test;
  • paired measurements → paired t-test;
  • three or more groups → ANOVA.

Those routes are developed in Chapter 7: t-Tests and Chapter 8: ANOVA.

Minimal decision rules:

  • use a t-test unless the distribution is strongly skewed or dominated by outliers;
  • use Welch rather than Student when group spreads differ clearly;
  • use Welch’s ANOVA rather than classical ANOVA when group spreads differ clearly;
  • use rank-based alternatives when the mean-based summary is no longer defensible.

If the model includes both a categorical predictor and a continuous covariate, you have moved beyond a pure comparison-of-means design and into an ANCOVA-type linear model. That belongs with Chapter 13: Multiple Regression and Model Specification.

4.2 Two or More Continuous Variables

Several closely related questions are possible here:

  • do two variables co-vary? → pairwise correlation;
  • how does a response change with one predictor? → simple linear regression;
  • how does a response depend on several predictors together? → multiple regression.

Use pairwise correlation when the aim is association between two variables and neither is being treated as the response. Use regression when one variable is the response and the other variable, or variables, are predictors.

That gives a practical progression:

  • two variables, association only → Chapter 9: Correlation and Association;
  • one response and one predictor → Chapter 11: Simple Linear Regression;
  • one response and several predictors → Chapter 13: Multiple Regression and Model Specification.

4.3 Binary or Proportional Response

For a single proportion compared with a reference value, or for two proportions compared between independent groups, the basic proportion-test family is usually enough. Once the probability of success depends on several predictors, repeated observations, sites, or any other grouped structure, move to generalised linear models, usually logistic regression.

The broader model-based designs begin in Chapter 19: Generalised Linear Models.

4.4 Count Response

Counts usually do not belong in the same family as mean-comparison tests. Use a model that respects count structure, such as a Poisson or negative-binomial GLM, rather than forcing counts into a Gaussian method.

This is taken further in Chapter 19: Generalised Linear Models.

4.5 Repeated, Clustered, or Hierarchical Data

If observations are repeated on the same individual, nested within sites, or clustered within some sampling structure, the basic independent-sample tests are no longer enough. This is a sampling or experimental design issue. The appropriate family is usually mixed-effects or hierarchical models.

Those designs are introduced in Chapter 17: Pseudoreplication and developed formally in Chapter 18: Dependence and Mixed Models.

5 Choosing by Question Type

This alternative route to deciding on a test to use matches how students may first describe a problem in words.

5.1 Is the Question About One Mean or One Proportion?

Use:

  • one-sample t-test for a continuous response and a reference mean;
  • Wilcoxon signed-rank when the one-sample t-test assumptions fail badly;
  • proportion tests for binary outcomes summarised as one proportion.

For continuous responses, see Chapter 7: t-Tests. For model-based binary-response work, continue to Chapter 19: Generalised Linear Models.

Example questions:

  • Is the mean tree height in this stand different from 23 m? → one-sample t-test
  • Is the proportion of infected plants different from 0.50? → one-sample proportion test
  • Is the typical chlorophyll concentration in this estuary different from a regulatory threshold when the data are too skewed for a mean-based test? → Wilcoxon signed-rank test

5.2 Is the Question About Two Groups?

Use:

  • two-sample t-test for two independent groups;
  • paired t-test for matched or before-after measurements;
  • rank-based Wilcoxon alternatives when the mean-based route is not defensible;
  • two-sample proportion tests for binary outcomes compared between two groups.

These two-group pathways are developed in Chapter 7: t-Tests. Binary-response extensions belong with Chapter 19: Generalised Linear Models.

Example questions:

  • Do Adelie and Chinstrap penguins differ in mean flipper length? → two-sample t-test
  • Did piglet mass change after treatment in the same individuals? → paired t-test
  • Do two treatment groups differ in germination success? → two-sample proportion test
  • Do two seed treatments differ in typical germination time when a few trays contain extreme delays? → Wilcoxon rank-sum test

5.3 Is the Question About Three or More Groups?

Use:

  • one-way ANOVA for one factor with three or more levels;
  • factorial ANOVA for two or more categorical factors;
  • Welch’s ANOVA when group spreads differ clearly;
  • Kruskal-Wallis when a rank-based one-way comparison is more defensible.

These multi-group routes are developed in Chapter 8: ANOVA.

Example questions:

  • Does mean body mass differ among the four penguin species? → one-way ANOVA
  • Do the four diet treatments differ in chick growth? → one-way ANOVA
  • Do temperature and salinity both affect response, and do they interact? → factorial ANOVA
  • Are group spreads clearly unequal across the five levels? → Welch’s ANOVA
  • Do parasite loads differ among three wetlands when the counts are strongly skewed? → Kruskal-Wallis test

5.4 Is the Question About Association Between Two Variables?

Use:

  • Pearson for linear association;
  • Spearman or Kendall for rank-based association;

Association without a response model is developed in Chapter 9: Correlation and Association.

Example questions:

  • Do canopy cover and soil moisture increase together? → Pearson correlation if the pattern is roughly linear
  • Do deeper kelp plants also tend to have longer stipes, even if the pattern is curved but ordered? → Spearman correlation
  • Do two observers give similar ordered condition scores to the same set of quadrats? → Kendall’s tau

5.5 Is the Question About Predicting or Explaining a Response?

Use:

  • simple linear regression when one response depends on one continuous predictor;
  • polynomial regression when the response-predictor pattern is curved;
  • multiple regression when one response depends on several predictors;
  • interaction models when the effect of one predictor depends on another;
  • GAMs when the response changes smoothly but not in a simple parametric form;
  • nonlinear regression when the response follows a mechanistic nonlinear curve.

These response-model routes are developed in Chapter 11: Simple Linear Regression, Chapter 12: Polynomial Regression, Chapter 13: Multiple Regression and Model Specification, Chapter 14: Interaction Effects, Chapter 20: Generalised Additive Models, and Chapter 21: Nonlinear Regression.

Example questions:

  • How does bill length change with body mass? → simple linear regression
  • Does growth rise and then level off with age? → polynomial regression or a GAM
  • How does abundance depend jointly on temperature, depth, and nitrate? → multiple regression
  • Does the effect of temperature differ between exposed and sheltered sites? → interaction model
  • Does enzyme activity follow a saturating mechanistic curve? → nonlinear regression

6 Assumptions and Alternatives

Assumptions are not all the same kind of thing. The most important split is:

  • independence → design issue;
  • distribution shape and outliers → data issue;
  • variance differences across groups → group-comparison issue.

Decide by:

  1. choosing the method family that matches the question and design;
  2. checking whether the assumptions of that family are plausible;
  3. if necessary, switching to a transformation, a rank-based alternative, or a more suitable model class.

For the core material covered so far, the main alternatives are:

  • one-sample or paired t-test → wilcox.test();
  • two-sample t-test → wilcox.test();
  • one-way ANOVA → oneway.test() or kruskal.test();
  • Pearson correlation → Spearman or Kendall correlation.

Assumption checking itself is handled in Chapter 6.

The test-specific applications appear in Chapter 7: t-Tests, Chapter 8: ANOVA, and Chapter 9: Correlation and Association.

7 Extending Beyond the Staple Tests

The staple tests are useful because they teach the reasoning behind method selection. They sit within a broader statistical toolbox.

Use model-based analysis when:

  • the response depends on multiple predictors;
  • the response is not Gaussian;
  • the design is hierarchical, repeated, or clustered;
  • the mean structure is curved or mechanistic rather than straight-line.

The major extensions are:

  • Chapter 11: Simple Linear Regression
  • Chapter 12: Polynomial Regression
  • Chapter 13: Multiple Regression and Model Specification
  • Chapter 19: Generalised Linear Models
  • Chapter 20: Generalised Additive Models
  • Chapter 21: Nonlinear Regression

The connection between test families is:

  • t-tests are the two-group members of the comparison-of-means family;
  • ANOVA extends that family to three or more groups;
  • regression extends the same linear-model approach to continuous predictors and multiple predictors.

8 Common Mistakes in Test Selection

These mistakes account for most bad method choices:

  • choosing a test because the function name is familiar rather than because the design fits;
  • ignoring whether the observations are paired or independent;
  • using correlation when the real aim is regression;
  • using many pairwise t-tests instead of ANOVA;
  • switching to a non-parametric test without understanding which assumption failed;
  • treating a binary response as if it were continuous;
  • acting as if there is a single correct test name rather than a correct model class.

9 Now Where?

Now that we have attended to the core (basic) statistical tests, we will look at modelling in the next chapters:

  • Chapter 11: Simple Linear Regression
  • Chapter 12: Polynomial Regression
  • Chapter 13: Multiple Regression and Model Specification

10 Summary

Choose a method in this order:

  1. start with the biological question;
  2. identify the response type and explanatory structure;
  3. decide whether observations are independent, paired, repeated, or nested;
  4. match the problem to the correct method family;
  5. check assumptions and use alternatives only for a clear reason;
  6. move to a broader model class when the response structure or design requires it;
  7. interpret the result through effect size and uncertainty, not through test name alone.

If you follow these steps, the correct method family usually becomes clear without memorising statistics as a disconnected list. Simply use this guide to point you to where you want to be.

Reuse

---
title: "10. Routes on the Map of the Statistical Landscape"
subtitle: "A Practical Decision Guide for Core Inferential Questions"
date: last-modified
date-format: "YYYY/MM/DD"
reference-location: margin
---

```{r code-brewing-opts, echo=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  comment = "R>",
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.asp = NULL,
  fig.align = "center",
  fig.retina = 2,
  dpi = 300
)

ggplot2::theme_set(
  ggplot2::theme_grey(base_size = 8)
)
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
## In This Chapter

-   how to connect the biological question to the method family
-   choosing among the core methods already introduced
-   a visual decision aid for moving from question to method
-   when to consider non-parametric alternatives
-   when the problem has moved beyond staple tests and into modelling
:::

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"}
## Tasks to Complete in This Chapter

-   None
:::

```{r code-libraries, echo=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
```

# Introduction

Many students first encounter statistics as a list of named tests. In practice, method selection starts with the biological question, the structure of the data, and the design of the study.

By this point you have already met the core tests. In your working life, you will have to choose among them and recognise when the problem has gone into a broader model class. But, test choice is only the first step. Good interpretation still depends on effect size, uncertainty, and biological context.

# The Decision Workflow

Use these steps first. The later sections provide two complementary ways to navigate it.

1. State the biological question.
2. Identify the response variable and its type.
3. Identify the explanatory structure: one sample, groups, paired data, or continuous predictor.
4. Decide whether the observations are independent, paired, repeated, or nested.
5. Choose the method family that matches the structure.
6. Check whether the assumptions of that family are plausible.
7. Interpret the result in terms of effect size, uncertainty, and the scientific question.

Test selection depends on the question, the data structure, the design, and the assumptions together, and no single R function can replace that decision flow order.

# Visual Decision Assistance

The workflow is summarised in @fig-method-selection-grid. Each row in the figure goes directly onto the categories developed in the next two sections.

```{r fig-method-selection-grid}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Links from common biological question types to the main method and the next extension when the problem becomes more complex."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5.5

decision_grid <- tribble(
  ~question, ~core_method, ~next_extension,
  "One mean or one proportion", "One-sample t-test or proportion test", "Wilcoxon signed-rank test or binomial / GLM extension",
  "Two independent groups", "Two-sample t-test", "Wilcoxon rank-sum test or regression / GLM",
  "Paired or before-after data", "Paired t-test", "Wilcoxon signed-rank test or mixed model",
  "Three or more groups", "ANOVA", "Kruskal-Wallis or factorial / mixed-model extension",
  "Two variables vary together", "Pearson / Spearman / Kendall correlation", "Regression if one variable is a response",
  "Continuous response with predictors", "Linear regression", "Polynomial, multiple regression, interactions",
  "Binary or proportional response", "Proportion tests (simple cases)", "GLMs: logistic or beta-style approaches",
  "Count response", "Not a core introductory test", "GLMs: Poisson / negative binomial",
  "Repeated or nested observations", "Not handled by core independent-sample tests", "Mixed models / hierarchical models",
  "Curved but smooth relationship", "Not handled well by straight-line models", "GAMs",
  "Mechanistic nonlinear response", "Not handled well by straight-line models", "Nonlinear regression / GNLM-style extensions"
) |>
  mutate(row = rev(row_number()))

tile_cols <- c(
  question = "#D9E6F2",
  core_method = "#E8F0D8",
  next_extension = "#F6E1C3"
)

bind_rows(
  decision_grid |> transmute(column = "Question Structure", row, text = question, fill = tile_cols["question"]),
  decision_grid |> transmute(column = "Core Choice", row, text = core_method, fill = tile_cols["core_method"]),
  decision_grid |> transmute(column = "Next Extension", row, text = next_extension, fill = tile_cols["next_extension"])
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = column, y = row)) +
  geom_tile(aes(fill = fill), colour = "white", width = 0.98, height = 0.95) +
  geom_text(aes(label = text), size = 2.8, lineheight = 0.95) +
  scale_fill_identity() +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL) +
  labs(x = NULL) +
  theme_grey() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  )
```

# Choosing by Data Structure

Usually, your data structure, often set at the experimental or sampling design stage, will show whether the simple core tests are enough or whether you need regression, GLMs, or mixed models.

## Continuous Response with a Categorical Predictor

This is the comparison-of-means family.

-   one sample against a reference value → one-sample *t*-test;
-   two independent groups → two-sample *t*-test;
-   paired measurements → paired *t*-test;
-   three or more groups → ANOVA.

Those routes are developed in [Chapter 7: *t*-Tests](07-t_tests.qmd) and [Chapter 8: ANOVA](08-anova.qmd).

Minimal decision rules:

-   use a *t*-test unless the distribution is strongly skewed or dominated by outliers;
-   use Welch rather than Student when group spreads differ clearly;
-   use Welch's ANOVA rather than classical ANOVA when group spreads differ clearly;
-   use rank-based alternatives when the mean-based summary is no longer defensible.

If the model includes both a categorical predictor and a continuous covariate, you have moved beyond a pure comparison-of-means design and into an ANCOVA-type linear model. That belongs with [Chapter 13: Multiple Regression and Model Specification](13-multiple-regression-and-model-specification.qmd).

## Two or More Continuous Variables

Several closely related questions are possible here:

-   do two variables co-vary? → pairwise correlation;
-   how does a response change with one predictor? → simple linear regression;
-   how does a response depend on several predictors together? → multiple regression.

Use pairwise correlation when the aim is association between two variables and neither is being treated as the response. Use regression when one variable is the response and the other variable, or variables, are predictors.

That gives a practical progression:

-   two variables, association only → [Chapter 9: Correlation and Association](09-correlation-and-association.qmd);
-   one response and one predictor → [Chapter 11: Simple Linear Regression](11-simple-linear-regression.qmd);
-   one response and several predictors → [Chapter 13: Multiple Regression and Model Specification](13-multiple-regression-and-model-specification.qmd).

## Binary or Proportional Response

For a single proportion compared with a reference value, or for two proportions compared between independent groups, the basic proportion-test family is usually enough. Once the probability of success depends on several predictors, repeated observations, sites, or any other grouped structure, move to generalised linear models, usually logistic regression.

The broader model-based designs begin in [Chapter 19: Generalised Linear Models](19-generalised-linear-models.qmd).

## Count Response

Counts usually do not belong in the same family as mean-comparison tests. Use a model that respects count structure, such as a Poisson or negative-binomial GLM, rather than forcing counts into a Gaussian method.

This is taken further in [Chapter 19: Generalised Linear Models](19-generalised-linear-models.qmd).

## Repeated, Clustered, or Hierarchical Data

If observations are repeated on the same individual, nested within sites, or clustered within some sampling structure, the basic independent-sample tests are no longer enough. This is a sampling or experimental design issue. The appropriate family is usually mixed-effects or hierarchical models.

Those designs are introduced in [Chapter 17: Pseudoreplication](17-pseudoreplication.qmd) and developed formally in [Chapter 18: Dependence and Mixed Models](18-dependence-and-mixed-models.qmd).

# Choosing by Question Type

This alternative route to deciding on a test to use matches how students may first describe a problem in words.

## Is the Question About One Mean or One Proportion?

Use:

-   one-sample *t*-test for a continuous response and a reference mean;
-   Wilcoxon signed-rank when the one-sample *t*-test assumptions fail badly;
-   proportion tests for binary outcomes summarised as one proportion.

For continuous responses, see [Chapter 7: *t*-Tests](07-t_tests.qmd). For model-based binary-response work, continue to [Chapter 19: Generalised Linear Models](19-generalised-linear-models.qmd).

Example questions:

-   Is the mean tree height in this stand different from `23 m`? → one-sample *t*-test
-   Is the proportion of infected plants different from `0.50`? → one-sample proportion test
-   Is the typical chlorophyll concentration in this estuary different from a regulatory threshold when the data are too skewed for a mean-based test? → Wilcoxon signed-rank test

## Is the Question About Two Groups?

Use:

-   two-sample *t*-test for two independent groups;
-   paired *t*-test for matched or before-after measurements;
-   rank-based Wilcoxon alternatives when the mean-based route is not defensible;
-   two-sample proportion tests for binary outcomes compared between two groups.

These two-group pathways are developed in [Chapter 7: *t*-Tests](07-t_tests.qmd). Binary-response extensions belong with [Chapter 19: Generalised Linear Models](19-generalised-linear-models.qmd).

Example questions:

-   Do Adelie and Chinstrap penguins differ in mean flipper length? → two-sample *t*-test
-   Did piglet mass change after treatment in the same individuals? → paired *t*-test
-   Do two treatment groups differ in germination success? → two-sample proportion test
-   Do two seed treatments differ in typical germination time when a few trays contain extreme delays? → Wilcoxon rank-sum test

## Is the Question About Three or More Groups?

Use:

-   one-way ANOVA for one factor with three or more levels;
-   factorial ANOVA for two or more categorical factors;
-   Welch's ANOVA when group spreads differ clearly;
-   Kruskal-Wallis when a rank-based one-way comparison is more defensible.

These multi-group routes are developed in [Chapter 8: ANOVA](08-anova.qmd).

Example questions:

-   Does mean body mass differ among the four penguin species? → one-way ANOVA
-   Do the four diet treatments differ in chick growth? → one-way ANOVA
-   Do temperature and salinity both affect response, and do they interact? → factorial ANOVA
-   Are group spreads clearly unequal across the five levels? → Welch's ANOVA
-   Do parasite loads differ among three wetlands when the counts are strongly skewed? → Kruskal-Wallis test

## Is the Question About Association Between Two Variables?

Use:

-   Pearson for linear association;
-   Spearman or Kendall for rank-based association;

Association without a response model is developed in [Chapter 9: Correlation and Association](09-correlation-and-association.qmd).

Example questions:

-   Do canopy cover and soil moisture increase together? → Pearson correlation if the pattern is roughly linear
-   Do deeper kelp plants also tend to have longer stipes, even if the pattern is curved but ordered? → Spearman correlation
-   Do two observers give similar ordered condition scores to the same set of quadrats? → Kendall's tau

## Is the Question About Predicting or Explaining a Response?

Use:

-   simple linear regression when one response depends on one continuous predictor;
-   polynomial regression when the response-predictor pattern is curved;
-   multiple regression when one response depends on several predictors;
-   interaction models when the effect of one predictor depends on another;
-   GAMs when the response changes smoothly but not in a simple parametric form;
-   nonlinear regression when the response follows a mechanistic nonlinear curve.

These response-model routes are developed in [Chapter 11: Simple Linear Regression](11-simple-linear-regression.qmd), [Chapter 12: Polynomial Regression](12-polynomial-regression.qmd), [Chapter 13: Multiple Regression and Model Specification](13-multiple-regression-and-model-specification.qmd), [Chapter 14: Interaction Effects](14-interaction-effects.qmd), [Chapter 20: Generalised Additive Models](20-generalised-additive-models.qmd), and [Chapter 21: Nonlinear Regression](21-nonlinear-regression.qmd).

Example questions:

-   How does bill length change with body mass? → simple linear regression
-   Does growth rise and then level off with age? → polynomial regression or a GAM
-   How does abundance depend jointly on temperature, depth, and nitrate? → multiple regression
-   Does the effect of temperature differ between exposed and sheltered sites? → interaction model
-   Does enzyme activity follow a saturating mechanistic curve? → nonlinear regression

# Assumptions and Alternatives

Assumptions are not all the same kind of thing. The most important split is:

-   **independence** → design issue;
-   **distribution shape and outliers** → data issue;
-   **variance differences across groups** → group-comparison issue.

Decide by:

1. choosing the method family that matches the question and design;
2. checking whether the assumptions of that family are plausible;
3. if necessary, switching to a transformation, a rank-based alternative, or a more suitable model class.

For the core material covered so far, the main alternatives are:

-   one-sample or paired *t*-test → `wilcox.test()`;
-   two-sample *t*-test → `wilcox.test()`;
-   one-way ANOVA → `oneway.test()` or `kruskal.test()`;
-   Pearson correlation → Spearman or Kendall correlation.

Assumption checking itself is handled in [Chapter 6](06-assumptions-and-transformations.qmd).

The test-specific applications appear in [Chapter 7: *t*-Tests](07-t_tests.qmd), [Chapter 8: ANOVA](08-anova.qmd), and [Chapter 9: Correlation and Association](09-correlation-and-association.qmd).

# Extending Beyond the Staple Tests

The staple tests are useful because they teach the reasoning behind method selection. They sit within a broader statistical toolbox.

Use model-based analysis when:

-   the response depends on multiple predictors;
-   the response is not Gaussian;
-   the design is hierarchical, repeated, or clustered;
-   the mean structure is curved or mechanistic rather than straight-line.

The major extensions are:

-   [Chapter 11: Simple Linear Regression](11-simple-linear-regression.qmd)
-   [Chapter 12: Polynomial Regression](12-polynomial-regression.qmd)
-   [Chapter 13: Multiple Regression and Model Specification](13-multiple-regression-and-model-specification.qmd)
-   [Chapter 19: Generalised Linear Models](19-generalised-linear-models.qmd)
-   [Chapter 20: Generalised Additive Models](20-generalised-additive-models.qmd)
-   [Chapter 21: Nonlinear Regression](21-nonlinear-regression.qmd)

The connection between test families is:

-   *t*-tests are the two-group members of the comparison-of-means family;
-   ANOVA extends that family to three or more groups;
-   regression extends the same linear-model approach to continuous predictors and multiple predictors.

# Common Mistakes in Test Selection

These mistakes account for most bad method choices:

-   choosing a test because the function name is familiar rather than because the design fits;
-   ignoring whether the observations are paired or independent;
-   using correlation when the real aim is regression;
-   using many pairwise *t*-tests instead of ANOVA;
-   switching to a non-parametric test without understanding which assumption failed;
-   treating a binary response as if it were continuous;
-   acting as if there is a single correct test name rather than a correct model class.

# Now Where?

Now that we have attended to the core (basic) statistical tests, we will look at modelling in the next chapters:

-   [Chapter 11: Simple Linear Regression](11-simple-linear-regression.qmd)
-   [Chapter 12: Polynomial Regression](12-polynomial-regression.qmd)
-   [Chapter 13: Multiple Regression and Model Specification](13-multiple-regression-and-model-specification.qmd)

# Summary

Choose a method in this order:

1. start with the biological question;
2. identify the response type and explanatory structure;
3. decide whether observations are independent, paired, repeated, or nested;
4. match the problem to the correct method family;
5. check assumptions and use alternatives only for a clear reason;
6. move to a broader model class when the response structure or design requires it;
7. interpret the result through effect size and uncertainty, not through test name alone.

If you follow these steps, the correct method family usually becomes clear without memorising statistics as a disconnected list. Simply use this guide to point you to where you want to be.
